Hey, welcome back. Alright, in this video I'm going to use this beautiful digital whiteboard to diagram how YouTube campaigns are structured. I'm going to be quick about it because this isn't really that exciting, but I want you to know how it's supposed to be done. Otherwise you might set it up in a way that drives you crazy and you might deal with a bunch of unnecessary unpleasantry. Alright, so here's how we do it. So in your YouTubes, it's very similar to Facebook in the sense that number one, we have a campaign. So I'll just like abbreviate there, CAM. And so within the campaign, this is where you're saying, okay YouTube, here's what I want you to do. I want you to go out and I want you to get me customers or leads or I want you to just get me video views or I want you to get me brand exposure or whatever, right? And that's where you're doing that. There's a couple of other options there. You can also tell it your bid. We'll talk about bids in a minute, but that's essentially where you're giving YouTube its directions. Now within a campaign, you have ad groups. Now if you're going to hear me mess this up like a million times, alright? So I'm always confused ad group and ad set. Ad group is YouTube, ad set is Facebook. So if you hear me screw it up, just bear with me. So at your ad group level, I'm just going to say AG, this is where you're telling the YouTube's a couple of things. You can tell them your bid again here, alright? So you could say, I normally willing to pay X amount per conversion or something. I'll show you that later. But more importantly, this is where you're defining your audience. And so within the world of YouTube, if we have audiences here, alright? So I'll just abbreviate this as AUD, there's all manner of targeting options that you've got. You can layer them and you can do all this mixing and matching stuff. And I think personally that it is way more accurate than Facebook. And what's also cool about YouTube is it still has its machine learning, which is pretty sweet, alright? So in terms of audiences, you have, I'll just call them pre-made audiences, right? Such as in-market audiences, like the ones I showed you. So YouTube can like know if someone is in the market for a new car just based on their browser history because it's Google and they know everything you're doing, right? So if you're like checking out Ford, Chevrolet, Googling, you know, F-150 pickup truck prices or whatever, you're going to be in that audience, which is pretty awesome. So there's a gazillion of those things, which is pretty sweet. So there's that type of audience. There's also keyword audiences, alright? Which is great. I love those. These are usually my default, okay? Because it's like YouTube will essentially say, alright, if people have searched for these keywords or keywords that are similar, so you got to watch that, it's not the exact keywords, they kind of get you there. You know, so if they've searched, like let's say you type in something like Facebook ads or whatever and you bid on that, they will do a, they'll like put in, you know, if someone typed in Facebook ads manager, they would include that, right? So it's that keyword or similar and you can read their little description there. But it's pretty dang cool. So you can literally be like, alright, you know, if someone is like searching for, you know, business consulting, advertising consultant, whatever, and you like come up with 10 or 30 different keywords, you can create an audience of people that are searching for those keywords and or similar and just show your ad to those people, which is really, really awesome. You can also do it by website visits, alright? So like you'll see me do, we'll build some test campaigns out here. If I wanted to create a campaign and I wanted to get people who are probably into marketing and probably have a real business, alright, I might target people who have visited HubSpot and or Infusionsoft or Mailchip or QuickFunnels or any other website that an actual online business owner would use, alright? And then you could also do that, you know, by competitor website. So I could say, okay, you know, now on the other one, I'll create another audience of people who go to websites like DigitalMarket or Brendan Burchard, mastermind.com, like all those other things. So it's really freaking cool in terms of what you can do. There are other audience types in here, but generally speaking, oh, and then obviously, sorry, there's of course, our retargeting. Good Lord. How could I forget that? And YouTube's retargeting is really, really amazing. So we can retarget stuff like website visit, alright? So someone's been to your website or something, and they'll be like, alright, show this video to everyone who's been to my website, like duh, okay? Obviously, we would want to do that. We can also show videos to people who have watched videos. So I'll do video views, and we can even narrow that down to specific videos, alright, that are on your channel, not necessarily other people's channels. You can do that, but that's telling you it's gimmicky, alright? So it's really, really amazing their level of targeting. So what we want to do is we want to, like in this ad group level, this is where we're like, okay, I want you to show this ad to people who are in the, again, the stands for pre-made audience, who are in market for whatever, marketing and advertising, right? And now we can even do overlays, and so we can say we want to show it to people who are in market for marketing and advertising, right? So I'll just put that as in market, right there, that's one of their pre-made audiences. And I also want them to have searched for specific keywords, right? We can do that as well, right? And if we wanted to, we could be obnoxious, but typically I don't want to throw too much stuff in there, but I would do like a demographic thing, I might say. And I also want them to be in the U.S., alright, just as an example, let's say, in the United States, and I want them to be, you know, between 35 and 50, so I'm just going to put A for age, and I want them to be in the top 30% of income, so I'm going to put income. And so we can actually set it up where your ad is only being shown to this little sweet spot right here, where these overlap. So there's like all manner of cool stuff you can do. Now just like anything, like the more granular we get with the targeting, the more expensive it is to show it, but there's something really cool about YouTube, which is this, if they skip your ad, you ain't going to pay for it. So when we get to the scripting part, it's really important to follow the directions in the scripting part, which is why YouTube ads are often way different from Facebook ads. We're trying to get their attention and hook them in the Facebook ads and stuff, because it doesn't matter. We're paying per 1000 impressions of our ad, whether they watch it or not. If they skip your ad before 30 seconds on YouTube, you're not going to get charged. So it's a huge difference there, but this at the ad group level, almost said ad set, ad group, this is where we tell YouTube, all right, this is who we want to show the ad to. That's primarily what we're doing there. And then finally, now we have the actual ad itself, all right? That is of critical importance. Really, really, really important. This is dramatically different than Facebook video ads. Typically, now I like to, as you might have seen in other trainings of mine, I like to try to get away with creating one that'll work on both platforms. So I'll usually follow a YouTube framework for Facebook ads, so I don't have to make two different ads. The video I showed you earlier was actually an ad that originally started on Facebook. I just threw it on YouTube and it happened to work. But generally speaking, for most people, you really have to make YouTube specific ads, all right, which is fine. Now the reason YouTube is so awesome, aside from all of this targeting, is if we're defaulting here to keyword-based stuff, you got to understand what YouTube really is, and that is YouTube's a giant search engine. I think the stats say it's the second biggest search engine in the world, or something, right? It's freaking huge. So that's what people are doing on YouTube a lot of time. They're going there to figure stuff out, right? So it's very, very powerful in that regard. So let me erase all of this stuff here. And then what we're going to do is I just want to tell you about your various methods of bidding. We're going to primarily do this, which is use two different types here, all right? So number one is max conversions, all right? And that's essentially the same thing as Facebook. Like, you know, with most, whoops, hit my microphone, with most Facebook ads, you just like let it go. You don't do any sort of custom bidding on your ad. You're like, all right, Facebook, you don't go out and spend this much per day and try to get me some sales and stuff. And that works pretty good. So max conversions is like that. You're like, all right, YouTube, just go out and try to get me some conversions. You know? And that's pretty cool. And like, sometimes that works great. So I'm just going to say, em, C. The next method is target cost per action. C. C. P. A. Where you're actually telling the YouTube's, all right, here's how much I'm willing to pay per conversion. And incidentally, they're going to like, you know, it's not going to be exact. So it's not as intense as Facebook in the sense that, you know, this really is going to be within 10%. Like they could go way over your target cost one day and also be way under your target cost. So, you know, there is that caveat to it. So you got to, you got to watch out. But the other part of it is, like you saw in that early video, if you've got to like, you know, a really competitive audience and you're bidding really high, you might not have to pay that much. Just like I showed you in mine, right? Like I bet, well, actually it's still here. Right? Just like I showed you in this guy, I was bidding 70 bucks per opt-in. All right? But I was paying 15 and look, it ended up making money. And this is a pre-made audience. So in this particular example at my ad set level, what I did was I just layered two things in that audience. Like, so number one, I said, all right, I'm willing to pay 70 bucks. And I layered my little circle tool, my drawing pen, all right? I layered in market over demographic, which was, I said, okay, everyone who's in the US and is in the top 10%, I think, of income. And I likely had some age restrictions because I typically don't show my ads to people that are under 30 or under 35 because they don't really, generally speaking, they don't have businesses, like real businesses, like who I try to attract. I'm not selling how to start a business or whatever. So that was that particular thing there. So these are our two. There's a third one, which is CPV, all right? And that's cost per view. So you can say, okay, YouTube, I'm willing to pay like a dollar per view for this video. And in that case, it's just going to show it. And the view is someone who's actually watched, I think it's 30 seconds. This is, well, like whatever the number is, it's going to change immediately after I hit finish on this recording. But I'm pretty sure it's 30 seconds, but they'll probably change that to 15 or eight or 20 or like 29 or something out of spite, right? But that's what CPV means. And all of these work. And so essentially, if you get into like really hardcore with YouTube and you're starting to spend a lot on YouTube, you'll end up using every single one of these bidding strategies. Likely we'll start here because this is just really simple. You're like, all right, let's go. Let's see what happens. And we'll let the machine learning do its thing.
